one_line_takeaway: MinervaRL, a hardness-gated answer-conditioned-rationale extension to GRPO that distills gold-label-seeded but label-free-inference traces for prompts with zero verifier reward, lifts mean score across 12 CTI benchmarks and 4 backbones from 48.2 (GRPO) to 52.5, outperforming SFT/rejection-finetuning/off-policy-RLVR baselines, while leaving dual-use risk proxies (WMDP-Cyber) roughly unchanged.
key_finding: Averaged across the four backbones, plain GRPO raises mean CTI score from 36.7 (base) to 48.2; MinervaRL raises it further to 52.5 (paper abstract states +15.8 points over base and +4.3 over GRPO on average, computed per-backbone-then-averaged). Per-backbone GRPO->MinervaRL average gains: Llama-3.1-8B 56.0->60.2 (+4.2), Llama-3.2-3B 42.1->48.3 (+6.2), Qwen3-8B 47.0->53.4 (+6.4), Qwen3-4B 47.6->48.0 (+0.4). On Llama-3.1-8B specifically, MinervaRL improves over the base model by +20.4 (RCM), +11.6 (VSP), +31.0 (ATE), +35.4 (RMS), +26.1 (ElasticRule) points. Against LUFFY-CTI (the strongest off-policy RLVR baseline), MinervaRL improves average score by +3.2 (Llama-3.1-8B), +2.9 (Llama-3.2-3B), +0.2 (Qwen3-8B), +4.0 (Qwen3-4B). On Llama-3.1-8B, MinervaRL (60.2 Avg.) exceeds external security-SFT models Llama-Primus-Merged (50.2) and Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct (51.9). MinervaRL reduces the 'zero-solve fraction' (share of prompts whose full rollout group scores 0) relative to GRPO across all four backbones, and reaches GRPO's best validation accuracy earlier, then continues improving beyond it (Figure 4). Increasing GRPO's rollout budget from N=8 to N=12 (Llama-3.1-8B) reduces zero-solve frequency but does not close the gap to MinervaRL (ablation Table 4a: GRPO 53.8 avg, GRPO-12-rollout 51.8 avg, MinervaRL 63.3 avg on Minerva-Dev/AthenaBench-Mini), indicating the gain is not merely from extra sampling. Answer-only SFT ablation improves Minerva-Dev (58.6) but degrades badly on AthenaBench-Mini (38.7, avg 48.7), underperforming MinervaRL (63.2/63.3, avg 63.3). Component ablations (no EMA teacher: 62.6 avg; no filtering: 62.2 avg; no ML/TextCNN filter: 61.6 avg) each underperform the full pipeline. Best distillation LR scale is gamma=0.05 (avg 63.3); gamma=0.01 gives 60.8, gamma=0.10 gives 59.1. On general-capability/dual-use checks (Llama-3.1-8B), MinervaRL preserves MMLU-Pro (43.6) and IFEval (73.8) relative to base/GRPO, improves CanaryExploit (28.4 vs 0.0 for GRPO and Foundation-Sec, 10.9 base), and keeps WMDP-Cyber flat relative to GRPO (47.1 vs 47.1; base 46.7). On text-to-SQL transfer (Qwen2.5-Coder-3B), MinervaRL attains the best average (60.8) across seven eval splits vs GRPO (60.3) and SQL-R1-3B (59.8). Reported training overhead: MinervaRL adds a 39.3% increase in a 10-step Llama-3.1-8B timing study.
core_contribution: The paper introduces (1) Minerva-CTI, a unified 16-task, 32,000-training/1,200-validation-instance dataset for cyber threat intelligence (CTI) built from MITRE ATT&CK, CAPEC, NVD, Mappings Explorer, and detection/emulation corpora (Sigma, Atomic Red Team, Microsoft Sentinel, Splunk Security Content), where every instance has a canonical, deterministically verifiable target (identifiers, identifier sets, or structured strings like CVSS v3.1 vectors); and (2) MinervaRL, a training method that augments standard GRPO-based RLVR with a 'hardness-gated answer-conditioned rationale' (ACR) mechanism: for prompts where no sampled rollout is fully verifier-correct (max reward < 1, excluding CVSS tasks), an EMA teacher is shown the gold label to generate a short rationale trace, the trace is filtered (verifier correctness + heuristic/TextCNN quality filter, threshold 0.5) and, if accepted, distilled back onto the original label-free prompt via a lightweight SFT update (up to M=256 pairs every I=10 steps, learning rate scaled by gamma=0.05). The deployed/inference-time prompt never contains the label. The paper also provides a formal 'expanding empirical support' argument (Section 4.3) that MinervaRL's ACR+distillation step should raise the probability of sampling a verifier-correct completion under fixed rollout budget k, converting previously all-zero reward groups into learnable ones.
threat_model: This is not an adversarial/attacker-defender threat-model paper; it is a defensive AI-capability paper (LLM post-training for CTI analyst workflows). No adversary model, attack surface, or exploit process is defined. The closest security-relevant framing is a dual-use/broader-impact statement: the authors note their CTI-automation capability could in principle lower the cost of generating offensive guidance, but state their training data and objectives are defensively oriented (indicators, vulnerabilities, mitigations) and expect models to be most useful to defensive CTI analysts. The paper also reports one dual-use-adjacent evaluation: WMDP-Cyber (a benchmark measuring 'malicious cyber use' knowledge) as a risk proxy, where MinervaRL does not increase the score relative to GRPO (47.1 vs 47.1 for Llama-3.1-8B-GRPO and MinervaRL, versus 46.7 for the base model).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) compute constraints meant only the distillation LR scale gamma was swept, leaving distillation interval I, per-interval cap M, and EMA decay alpha unswept; (2) Minerva-CTI and the evaluation suite are primarily English-centric, so multilingual/regional CTI is untested; (3) ACR trace filtering relies on lightweight heuristics plus a TextCNN classifier rather than a stronger (but costlier) LLM-based filter; (4) MinervaRL adds real training overhead (39.3% increase in a 10-step timing study) from ACR generation, log-prob computation, and periodic distillation. Additional caveats visible in the results themselves: gains are backbone-dependent and small for Qwen3-4B (+0.4 avg over GRPO); on Llama-3.1-8B the human/GPT-5.2 preference evaluation actually ranks STaR-CTI and DART-CTI above MinervaRL for response quality, even though MinervaRL wins on verifier-scored accuracy; and full train/test contamination cannot be ruled out given shared public CTI standards, addressed only partially (temporal CVE splits, excluding MC formats from training, distinct evaluation-only benchmarks) rather than eliminated.

datasets_used: Minerva-CTI (32,000 training instances / 1,200 validation instances, 16 tasks, built from MITRE ATT&CK, MITRE CAPEC, NVD, Mappings Explorer, Sigma, Atomic Red Team, Microsoft Sentinel content, Splunk Security Content)
SOCEval (multi-select SOC-style reasoning over threat-intel reports)
VSP (CVSS v3.1 base-vector prediction, from AthenaBench)
ATE (ATT&CK technique identification from scenarios, from AthenaBench)
ElasticRule (Elastic detection-rule to ATT&CK technique mapping)
MMLU-Pro, IFEval, CanaryExploit, WMDP-Cyber (general/dual-use auxiliary evals)
Spider Dev/Test, BIRD Dev, DK, Syn, Real, Live (text-to-SQL transfer eval suite, via SQL-R1 setup)
quantitative_results: Average across 4 backbones: base 36.7 -> GRPO 48.2 -> MinervaRL 52.5
Llama-3.1-8B MinervaRL vs base: RCM +20.4, VSP +11.6, ATE +31.0, RMS +35.4, ElasticRule +26.1 points
Llama-3.1-8B-MinervaRL 60.2 avg vs Llama-Primus-Merged 50.2 and Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct 51.9
Ablation (Minerva-Dev/AthenaBench-Mini avg): Base 30.6, GRPO 53.8, GRPO-12-rollout 51.8, SFT(answer-only) 48.7, MinervaRL 63.3, no-EMA-teacher 62.6, no-filtering 62.2, no-ML-filter 61.6

Training-aligned vs not-in-train task avg (Table 2): Llama-3.1-8B base 37.1/54.4, GRPO 53.0/57.5, MinervaRL 61.7/59.5; Llama-3.2-3B base 4.9/47.2, GRPO 31.5/47.4, MinervaRL 46.4/49.3; Qwen3-8B base 35.4/38.7, GRPO 40.3/50.3, MinervaRL 49.1/55.5; Qwen3-4B base 33.2/24.8, GRPO 43.2/49.8, MinervaRL 42.6/50.7

General/cyber benchmarks (Llama-3.1-8B family): MMLU-Pro base 44.1/GRPO 45.6/MinervaRL 43.6; IFEval base 72.3/GRPO 72.3/MinervaRL 73.8; CanaryExploit base 10.9/GRPO 0.0/MinervaRL 28.4; WMDP-Cyber base 46.7/GRPO 47.1/MinervaRL 47.1
Text-to-SQL transfer avg (7 splits): Qwen2.5-Coder base 58.8, SQL-R1-3B 59.8, GRPO 60.3, MinervaRL 60.8
GPT-5.2 judge validation vs humans: QWK 0.6313 / Spearman 0.7722 (vs human avg), human-human QWK 0.6514 / Spearman 0.6461, vs Claude Sonnet 4.6 QWK 0.7634 / Spearman 0.8266
Training overhead: +39.3% wall-clock in a 10-step Llama-3.1-8B timing study
Minerva-CTI label-space scale note: January 2026 snapshot has 944 CWE identifiers and 44 MITRE Enterprise mitigation identifiers
baselines_compared: Unadapted base models (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Qwen3-4B-Base, Qwen3-8B-Base)
Standard GRPO (same Minerva-CTI data/verifiers, no ACR)
GRPO with increased rollout budget (N=12, Llama-3.1-8B only)
STaR-CTI (iterative verifier-bootstrapped SFT, Zelikman et al. 2022 style)
DART-CTI (rejection-finetuning with fixed verifier-filtered corpus, Tong et al. 2024 style)
LUFFY-CTI (off-policy RLVR with one accepted guidance trace per prompt, Yan et al. 2025 style)
Answer-only SFT (direct final-answer imitation, no rationale)
External security-SFT reference models: Llama-Primus-Merged, Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct
